cases.title: Shaky and Sweaty
cases.presentation: A 29-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes is found shaky, diaphoretic, and anxious after skipping breakfast before a workout. She is alert and able to swallow safely.
cases.question: What is the appropriate field treatment?
options: A: IM glucagon, since she is diabetic.
B: Oral glucose, since she is alert, cooperative, and able to swallow safely.
C: IV dextrose only, bypassing oral options.
D: No treatment needed since symptoms are mild.
cases.explanation: This is mild hypoglycemia in an alert patient with intact airway reflexes who can safely swallow — oral glucose is the appropriate first-line treatment rather than IM glucagon or IV dextrose, which are reserved for patients who cannot safely take oral glucose.
cases.clinicalPearl: If they're awake and can swallow safely, oral glucose is simpler and just as effective as IV or IM routes.

cases.title: Vomiting With a Near-Normal Glucose
cases.presentation: A 41-year-old man with type 2 diabetes on an SGLT2-inhibitor medication presents with two days of vomiting, abdominal pain, and unusually deep, rapid breathing. His glucose reads 175 mg/dL.
cases.question: What should this presentation raise suspicion for, despite the near-normal glucose?
options: A: This cannot be a diabetic emergency since the glucose is not significantly elevated.
B: Euglycemic DKA, given the SGLT2-inhibitor use, Kussmaul-pattern breathing, fruity breath odor, and vomiting/abdominal pain.
C: Simple viral gastroenteritis unrelated to his diabetes.
D: HHS, given his type 2 diabetes history.
cases.explanation: SGLT2-inhibitor use combined with Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath odor, and vomiting/abdominal pain is the classic presentation of euglycemic DKA — a normal or near-normal glucose does not rule out DKA in this context, and roughly 10% of DKA presents this way.
cases.clinicalPearl: SGLT2 inhibitor plus a DKA-looking picture with a normal-ish glucose is the textbook setup for euglycemic DKA — don't let the glucose number reassure you.

cases.title: Days of Decline
cases.presentation: An 78-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes has become progressively more confused and lethargic over three days, with poor oral intake and profound dry mucous membranes. Her glucose reads 720 mg/dL.
cases.question: What does this presentation most likely represent, and what is the field management priority?
options: A: DKA — administer field insulin immediately.
B: HHS — provide supportive care (oxygen, IV fluids per protocol, monitoring), protect the airway, and transport rapidly for definitive hospital treatment.
C: Simple hypoglycemia — administer oral glucose.
D: A primary psychiatric condition unrelated to her diabetes.
cases.explanation: Severe hyperglycemia, profound dehydration, slower multi-day onset, prominent obtundation, and a near-normal respiratory pattern (rather than Kussmaul) in an older type 2 diabetic patient are classic for HHS rather than DKA. Field management is supportive care and rapid transport — insulin administration is a hospital-level intervention, not a field one.
cases.clinicalPearl: Slow onset, severe dehydration, profound confusion, and sky-high glucose without Kussmaul breathing points toward HHS, not DKA — support and transport, don't try to fix the glucose in the field.
